coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light driven photocatalytic activity for 4-chlorophenol degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yun
Zhang, Jun
Zhou, Dandan
Dong, Shuangshi
coredata.dc:description: A series of metal silver (Ag) and carbon dots (CDots) co-doped BiOBr ternary systems (Ag/CDots/BiOBr) were successfully prepared by a two-step method. The CDots and Ag nanoparticles were successively introduced into BiOBr with the ionic liquid (IL) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([C16mim]Br) as the precursor. The sphere-like structure of the as-prepared ternary photocatalyst was 1–2 μm in diameter. The compositional characterization and optical and electrical properties of photocatalysts were investigated in detail. The prepared ternary photocatalysts possessed the excellent visible-light driven photocatalytic degradation for 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) with Ag content at 0.9 wt% and CDots content at 2 wt%. Additionally, the 4-CP removal efficiencies decreased in the order of 0.9Ag/2CDots/BiOBr (88.58%) > 0.9Ag/BiOBr (68.12%) > 2CDots/BiOBr (62.12%) > BiOBr (47.15%) under visible light illumination in 6 h. The dissolved organic carbon (DOC) removal and the dechlorination efficiency by the Ag/CDots/BiOBr were 62.3% and 81.5%, much higher than those by the pristine BiOBr. The coexistence of Ag and CDots on the BiOBr promoted visible light harvesting capability and prevent the recombination of photo-generated electron/hole pairs. The Ag/CDots/BiOBr photocatalytic mechanism was discussed by band structure analysis and free radical trapping experiments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDots
Ag
BiOBr
Ternary photocatalyst
Band structure
4-CP

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a), FT-IR spectra (b) of as-prepared BiOBr, Ag/BiOBr, CDots/BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr samples.
XPS spectra of the Ag/CDots/BiOBr composites: full survey (a), Bi 4f (b), Br 3d (c), O 1s (d), C 1s (e), Ag 3d (f).
SEM (a), HRTEM (b) images and EDS mapping (c–h) of the as-prepared Ag/CDots/BiOBr photocatalysts.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm of (a) BiOBr, (b) CDots/BiOBr, (c) Ag/BiOBr and (d) Ag/CDots/BiOBr.

UV–vis DRS (a) and calculated the band gaps (b) of as-prepared the BiOBr, Ag/BiOBr, CDots/BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr. Valence band XPS spectra (c) and band structure diagram (d) of the BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr photocatalysts.

Photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP (a), the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model (b) of the as-prepared BiOBr and CDots/BiOBr. Photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP (c), the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model (d) of the as-prepared Ag/BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr. The released Cl− and DOC removal efficiency for 4-CP of the BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr (e), cycling test of Ag/CDots/BiOBr (f).
Transient photocurrent curves (a), EIS (b), PL spectra (c) of BiOBr, CDots/BiOBr, Ag/BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr photocatalysts under visible light irradiation.

Free radical trapping experiments of active-species during the photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP (a) Schematic photocatalytic mechanism of the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges (b) in the Ag/CDots/BiOBr system under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light driven photocatalytic activity for 4-chlorophenol degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, Jilin, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of metal silver (Ag) and carbon dots (CDots) co-doped BiOBr ternary systems (Ag/CDots/BiOBr) were successfully prepared by a two-step method. The CDots and Ag nanoparticles were successively introduced into BiOBr with the ionic liquid (IL) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([C16mim]Br) as the precursor. The sphere-like structure of the as-prepared ternary photocatalyst was 1–2 μm in diameter. The compositional characterization and optical and electrical properties of photocatalysts were investigated in detail. The prepared ternary photocatalysts possessed the excellent visible-light driven photocatalytic degradation for 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) with Ag content at 0.9 wt% and CDots content at 2 wt%. Additionally, the 4-CP removal efficiencies decreased in the order of 0.9Ag/2CDots/BiOBr (88.58%) > 0.9Ag/BiOBr (68.12%) > 2CDots/BiOBr (62.12%) > BiOBr (47.15%) under visible light illumination in 6 h. The dissolved organic carbon (DOC) removal and the dechlorination efficiency by the Ag/CDots/BiOBr were 62.3% and 81.5%, much higher than those by the pristine BiOBr. The coexistence of Ag and CDots on the BiOBr promoted visible light harvesting capability and prevent the recombination of photo-generated electron/hole pairs. The Ag/CDots/BiOBr photocatalytic mechanism was discussed by band structure analysis and free radical trapping experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic techniques had attracted explosive attention since it could utilize the solar energy to degrade the contaminants efficiently, as well as its low cost, innocuous, environment-friendly and easily available nature [1,2]. The diverse photocatalysts were studied for the degradation of organic pollutants under Ultra Violet (UV) or visible light irradiation such as TiO2 [3], ZnO [4], WO3 [5], g-C3N4 [6], ZnFe2O4 [7] and Bi2WO6 [8]. Brominated oxygen bismuth (BiOBr) had unique layered structure built by interlacing [Bi2O2]2+ slabs with double Br atoms, which was benefit to form internal static electric fields between Br positive and Br negative layer and then enhanced separation of the photo-generated electron/hole pairs [9,10]. Besides, the photo-excited electrons should travel a certain k-space distance to the valence band due to the indirect-transition band-gap of BiOBr, leading to the low recombination probability of the electron/hole pairs [11]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance of unitary BiOBr was still limited owing to the low solar energy utilization efficiency and rapid electron/hole recombination [12].

In recent years, carbon dots (a carbon nanomaterial < 10 nm in diameter) attracted the interests of researchers due to the unique characteristics over conventional semiconductor-based dots including water-soluble, intrinsic low toxicity, good biocompatibility, photostable and nanosized light-harvester [13]. The conjugated π structure of the CDots made them excellent electron reservoir and transporter [14]. Simultaneously, the up conversion properties of CDots enabled the light from the longer wavelength to shorter wavelength, improving the utilization of solar light [5,12]. These properties endowed CDots as a co-catalyst to decorate many semiconductors to accelerate the separation–transportation process of photo-induced carriers within the semiconductors and improve their interfacial reduction/oxidation reactions [7,15,16].

The noble metal nanoparticle (such as Pt, Pd, Ag, Au) could dramatically extend the light harvesting range and accelerate effective separation of electron/hole pairs [17]. Among them, silver (Ag) was relatively low in cost and toxicity [18]. The Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) effect of Ag nanoparticles could cause intense local electromagnetic fields and improved visible light absorption capability, which then could promote photocatalytic activity [19,20]. Therefore, the Ag modified semiconductor photocatalysts exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance in visible light regions [20–23].

Recently, the ternary photocatalysts with Ag and CDots co-dopants have received much attention owing to much higher photocatalytic performance compared to the binary photocatalysts [8,24,25]. However, limited work has been done on the mechanism insight to the combined effect of Ag and CDots on the crystal-facet of semiconductors. The appropriate band gap with proper matching of the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) levels plays an important role for a photocatalyst to degrade pollutants in water [5]. Some researchers found that the introduction of Ag or CDots to the semiconductor led to the change of band structure [5,20,23], while Di et al. [26] and Luo et al. [7] claimed no change in their study. Thus, it is important to investigate the effect of Ag and CDots co-doped on the band structure of semiconductors.

In this work, we fabricated the Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary photocatalyst to dramatically improve the photocatalytic activity compared to the pristine BiOBr and the binary system. The crystal structures, morphologies and optical and electrochemical properties were investigated in detail. The photocatalytic activities of the Ag/CDots/BiOBr microspheres were tested by the degradation of 4-CP. The effect of Ag and CDots on the band structure of BiOBr microspheres was intensively studied. Accordingly, the photocatalytic mechanism of the separation and transfer of photo-generated charges on the Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary system was proposed and elucidated under visible light illumination.

All the chemicals were used without further purification. CDots were synthesized according to our previous report [5]. The CDots/BiOBr were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method which was reported by Cheng et al. [27]. In a typical procedure, 4 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into 80 mL EGME (ethylene glycol monomethylether) to obtain solution A. 6 mmol IL [C16mim]Br and a certain amount of CDots (4 mg/L) were dispersed into 80 mL EGME to obtain solution B. The solution B was added into solution A dropwise under stirring condition and then the mixing solution was stirred for another 30 min. The mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 2 h and then cooled down to room temperature naturally. Subsequently, the resultant product was centrifuged and washed adequately with alcohol and deionized water three times. Finally, the resulting precipitates were dried in a vacuum at 60 °C overnight and ground into fine powder before further characterization and using in photocatalytic reactions. A certain volume of CDots in the CDots/BiOBr binary system were 0, 1, 2, 4, 8 mL, which were accordingly denoted as BiOBr and xCDots/BiOBr (x: the weight ratios of the CDots, x = 0.5, 1, 2 and 4), respectively. Preliminary experiments indicated that the composite synthesized by addition of 2 wt% CDots solution displayed the highest photocatalytic activity among the binary photocatalysts (Fig. 6a). Thus, this condition was selected to further synthesize the ternary composites.

A certain amount of Ag added contents on the Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary system were synthesized by a modified reduction method [21]. Firstly, 1 g as-prepared 2CDots/BiOBr powders were dispersed in 100 mL deionized water that containing a certain amount of AgNO3 under vigorous stirring. Then, various amount of NaBH4 solution was added dropwise into the suspension under stirring and further stirred for 30 min. Then the target product was collected after centrifugation, washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times, and dried in the oven at 60 °C overnight. The content of Ag (AgNO3 as a source of Ag) was chosen as 0.3, 0.6, 0.9 and 1.5 wt%, which were named as yAg/2CDots/BiOBr (y: the weight ratios of Ag, y = 0.3, 0.6, 0.9 and 1.5), respectively. To further understand the roles of Ag on the photocatalytic activities, Ag/BiOBr (Ag content of 0.9 wt%) was prepared with BiOBr as precursor following the same procedure stated as above according to the result of the Fig. 6c.

Crystallographic information was examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) under a Bruker D8 (Germany) diffractometer equipped with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) and the scanning range was from 10 o to 80 o. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Spectrum One, Perkin Elmer) were measured on a Nicolet 500 infrared spectrometer using KBr pellet technique. The surface elemental chemical state of the as-prepared samples were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, USA) using monochromated Al Kα X-ray (1486.6 eV) as the excitation source radiation. The size and morphology of the synthesized samples were estimated by recording images on the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7001F), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, via a 200 kV FEI-Tecnai F20, USA). The chemical composition was investigated via energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface and pore structure were determined by N2 adsorption at 77 K using an ASAP 2020 automatic analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument, USA). The light absorption ability was performed by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS, Shimadzu UV-2550, Japan), employed BaSO4 power as the reflectance standard in the experiment. All binding energies were referenced to carbon at 284.6 eV. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the samples was detected using a fluorescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian Medical Systems, USA).

The visible light photocatalytic activity of the obtained catalysts materials were investigated by the photodegradation of 4-CP. A 150 W Xe lamp equipped with a UV cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm) was used for visible light irradiation and the distance between the model pollutant and the light was fixed at 15 cm. In a typical procedure, 0.1 g photocatalyst powder was dispersed into 100 mL of the 4-CP (20 mg/L) solution. Before visible light illumination, the suspension was magnetically stirred in the dark for 3 h to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. A given volume of suspension solution sample was taken out by a syringe at given intervals and then was centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst particles. The reusability and stability of the photocatalysts was measured via repeating the identical experiment for five more times. The 4-CP concentration was analyzed on a high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1100 infinity, Agilent Corporation, USA) equipped with a reverse-phase column (Agilent Eclipse XDB-C18) with 150 mm length and 4.6 mm internal diameter. 4-CP was quantified at λ = 280 nm. The mobile phase consisted of 70% acetonitrile and 30% water at a flow rate of 1 mL/min. 20 μL of the sample solution was injected. The chloride ion was determined by an ion chromatograph (Diane ICS-2100, USA). Besides, the dissolved organic carbon (DOC) of 4-CP was carried out by a total organic carbon analyzer (SSM-5000A, Shimadzu Company).

To investigate the photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) properties, the as-prepared modified electrodes were prepared. 5 mg of the sample was suspended in 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol to produce suspension, which was then spin-coated (2000 rpm) onto the FTO glass electrode. The photocurrent and EIS measurement were performed on a CHI660B electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China) in a standard three-electrode system with a Pt wire as a counter electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode and FTO as working electrode, as well as 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the supporting electrolyte. A 150 W Xe lamp equipped with a UV cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm) was utilized as the light source on the photocurrent and EIS measurement.

In the scavenger quenching experiments, superoxide radicals (O2 −), hydroxyl radical (OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), holes (h+) and electrons (e−) were investigated by adding scavengers 2 mM 4-Hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinoxyl (TEMPOL, Sigma, 97%) for (a quencher of O2 −), 0.5 mM isopropanol (a quencher of OH), 0.1 mM Fe(II)-EDTA (a quencher of H2O2), 0.5 mM sodium oxalate (a quencher of hole) and 0.05 mM sodium chromate (VI) (a quencher of electrons).

The crystal structure and phase composition of the as-prepared BiOBr, Ag/BiOBr, CDots/BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr materials were characterized by XRD (Fig. 1a). The positions of peaks for all the samples were readily indexed to the structure of the tetragonal phase of BiOBr (JCPDS card No. 73-2061). The intensity of diffraction peak (2θ = 32.27°) for the crystal plane (110) increased with the doping of Ag and/or CDots, indicating that Ag and/or CDots had placed into the lattice and affected the growth rate of the crystal [12]. It was worth noting that the peak at around 44.30° occurred in Ag/BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr hybrid composites, suggesting that Ag particles had been successfully introduced into the composites [21,28]. Also, no diffraction peak of CDots (2θ = 26°) could be found in the CDots/BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr, which might be ascribed to its low loading amount with uniform dispersion in the samples that may be beyond the XRD detection limit. This result can also be found in similar systems [8,29]. No extra characteristic peaks from possible impurities could be observed, indicating the high purity and single phase of the as prepared composites.

Fig. 1b displayed the FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared composites. The absorption band at 514 cm−1 was ascribed to the BiO stretching mode [30,31]. The peak at about 1400 cm−1 might have been caused by the existence of OH (COOH/H2O), COH, COO−, and CH (the methyl group) stretching vibrations after the introduction Ag and/or CDots on the BiOBr [5,12,19,32]. The absorption peak located at 1654 cm−1 might be due to the bending vibrations of CO (COOH) and OH (the free water molecules) in the Ag/CDots/BiOBr system [32,33]. These results indicated that the existence of COOH and OH groups on the surface of CDots. The absorption peak at 3130 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH of adsorbed water molecules [34].

To illustrate the surface elemental compositions and their valence states, the XPS analysis of the Ag/CDots/BiOBr composite was carried out. The full survey spectrum (Fig. 2a) showed that the as-prepared was composed of Bi, Br, O, C and Ag elements. Fig. 2b shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Bi 4f spectra, where two peaks at 158.5 eV and 163.8 eV could be indexed with Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively. Meanwhile the binding energy between these two peaks was 5.3 eV, indicating the presence of the Bi3+ in the composite, which agreed well with previous reports [10,35]. From Br 3d spectra (Fig. 2c), it can be found that two strong peaks of the spin-orbit splitting energy at 67.9 eV and 68.7 eV, which were associated to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2, respectively, which verified that the bromine species were Brˉ anions [12,36]. As could be seen from Fig. 2c, the peak of C 1s binding energies at 284.6, 285.1 and 286.0 eV can be ascribed to sp2 CC/CC bonds, CO/CO and COC bond, indicating the formation of CDots [8,14,24,33]. For the O 1s (Fig. 1e), two peaks at about 529.6 eV and 530.1 eV could be ascribed to the oxygen in Bi2O2 2+ and BiO chemical bonding in the BiOBr, respectively [36,37]. The other peaks centered at 531.4 eV, 532.3 eV and 533.5 eV binding energies, corresponded to different types of oxygen molecule such as hydroxyl groups, crystal water, carboxyl groups [24,36–38]. In Fig. 1f, the Ag 3d spectrum showed the binding energy at 368.2 eV for Ag 3d5/2 and 374.1 eV for Ag 3d3/2, respectively, corresponding to the metallic Ag [10,17].

The surface morphology of the as-synthesized Ag/CDots/BiOBr materials was detected by the field emission SEM and the high-resolution TEM. In Fig. 3a, it can be seen that the as-prepared composites presented the sphere-like structure with an average diameter of about 1–2 μm. The high-magnification SEM image illustrated that the edge of sphere-like structure was constructed of plentiful ultrathin nanosheets with the thickness at approximate 10 nm (Fig. 3a inset). The unique porous structure with the accumulation of nanosheets favored the faster interfacial charge transfer, which was benefit for the photodegradation [39]. The formation of Ag/CDots/BiOBr composite was further proved by high-resolution TEM (Fig. 3b). The observed lattice fringe spacing of 0.277 nm matched the (110) crystallographic plane of BiOBr crystal [29,31]. The lattice fringe spacing with d = 0.212 nm was in accordance with the crystal plane of CDots (101) facet, demonstrating the successful coupling of the CDots with BiOBr [16]. Additionally, the lattice spacing at 0.238 nm could be observed, which corresponded to the (111) crystal plane of metal Ag particles [40,41]. These results intimated that CDots and Ag were uniformly dispersed on the surface of BiOBr nanosheets. As shown in Fig. 3c–h, the EDS mapping clearly presented the elements of Bi, O, Br, C and Ag were well distributed on the as-synthesized Ag/CDots/BiOBr materials. Therefore, the above results together demonstrated that the CDots and metallic Ag are successfully introduced on the surface of BiOBr materials.

The surface area and pore structure of BiOBr, CDots/BiOBr, Ag/BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr were investigated by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. 4 ). Additionally, the pore size distribution of the as-prepared samples was determined using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method (inset in Fig. 4). According to IUPAC classification of mesoporous materials, the as-prepared samples with the hysteresis loop in the P/P0 range of 0.00–1.00 exhibited typical type IV isotherms [2]. As shown in Fig. 4, the mesopores in the size range of 2–50 nm were observed, which might be ascribed to ultrathin nanosheets of the sphere-like structure [42]. From Fig. 4a–b, the BET surface areas of CDots/BiOBr (29.69 m2/g) was higher than that of pristine BiOBr (24.8 m2/g), which might be due to the fact that the CDots as adsorption centers center could adsorb more reactants and active species on the surface and then improve photocatalytic activity [31]. However, the BET surface areas of Ag/BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr were 20.03 and 21.08 m2/g, respectively (Fig. 4c–d). These might be ascribed to the modification of Ag and/or CDots occupied large spaces of BiOBr [2,17,26]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the BET surface areas were not the key factor affecting the photocatalytic performance in the Ag/CDots/BiOBr system.

Fig. 5a illustrated the UV–vis DRS of as-prepared composites. The absorption band with the onset absorption band edge of pristine BiOBr was approximately located at 430 nm due to the intrinsic band gap transition rather than the impurity level transition [29]. The absorption of visible-light (430 to 800 nm) for the binary (CDots/BiOBr and Ag/BiOBr) and ternary catalysts (Ag/CDots/BiOBr) significantly increased with the strongest capacity in Ag/CDots/BiOBr. The absorption edges for binary and ternary catalysts exhibited the slight red-shift compared to that for the pristine BiOBr. The results suggested that the introduction of Ag and/or CDots enable the composites to absorb more visible light and to endow the formation of more photo-excited electron/hole pairs with improved the photocatalytic activity [2,8]. Fig. 5b showed the energy band gaps of the samples, which were calculated by the classical Tauc's approach [43,44]. The band gap (Eg) of Ag/CDots/BiOBr (2.77 eV) was smaller than those of CDots/BiOBr (2.90 eV), Ag/BiOBr (2.82 eV) and BiOBr (2.92 eV). The narrower band gap was, the higher electrons transfer rate would be, and hence a better photocatalytic performance in visible light [12,16,23]. The VB of BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr were measured using XPS valence spectra (Fig. 5c) with the empirical equation [18,39]. The VB maximum positions (EVB) of pristine BiOBr and Ag/CDots/BiOBr were determined to be both 3.06 eV, indicating that the modification of Ag and CDots would not affect the electronic structure of BiOBr. Besides, the existing form of Ag and CDots were on the surface for charge transfer instead of inserting into the crystal lattice of BiOBr [42]. According to the formula ECB = EVB − Eg and combined with the band gap, the CB of Ag/CDots/BiOBr (ECB = 0.29 eV) was higher than the CB of pure BiOBr (ECB = 0.14 eV), as showed in Fig. 5d. In fact, the more negative CB for photocatalyst endowed more strong reduction capacity and improved the degradation efficiencies of organic contaminants [34]. Above all, the modification of Ag and CDots improved visible light harvesting capability and showed the slight red shift of absorption band edges, as well as reduced the band region. On the one hand, the up conversion ability of CDots, as a superior light harvester, could utilize more visible light [2]. On the other hand, the enhancement of photo-absorption could be attributed to the SPR effect of the Ag nanoparticles [18,19].

The optimal doping amount of CDots in CDots/BiOBr and that of Ag in Ag/CDots/BiOBr were successively determined by the 4-CP degradation experiments under visible light illumination. The light source showed no removal of the 4-CP from Fig. 6a. Prior to irradiation, it could be seen that the dark absorption–desorption equilibrium between the catalyst and 4-CP was achieved in 2 h. As to the pristine BiOBr, the degradation efficiency of 4-CP was 47.15% in 6 h. Notably, the CDots/BiOBr composite with the 2 wt% of CDots exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity in all binary catalysts, where 62.12% of the 4-CP was decomposed in 6 h. The photocatalytic efficiency monotonously ascended with the increase of CDots contents in CDots/BiOBr, while, the catalytic activity decreased when the deposition increased to 4%. The possible reason is that the light scattering and non-transparent of excessive CDots blocked the absorption of visible light and reduced the number of electron-hole pairs [7,39]. To quantitatively understand the reaction kinetics of 4-CP degradation, the rate constants (k) were calculated from the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model [44,45]. From Fig. 6b, the rate constant reached at the highest value of 0.1503 h−1 when the CDots contents was 2 wt% in the CDots/BiOBr, which was approximately 1.5 times than that of pristine BiOBr (0.1004 h−1).

At this CDots contents, Ag was then introduced to construct the tertiary composite for further higher photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 6c, the removal efficiencies of 4-CP decreased in the order of 0.9Ag/2CDots/BiOBr (88.58%) > 1.5Ag/2CDots/BiOBr (80.57%) > 0.6Ag/2CDots/BiOBr (77.43%) > 0.3Ag/2CDots/BiOBr (69.27%) > 0.9Ag/BiOBr (68.12%) in 6 h. It was a worth noting that the photocatalytic activity began to decrease due to the extra Ag forming carrier recombination center [8]. Meanwhile, the calculated k value for Ag/CDots/BiOBr with 0.9 wt% Ag was 0.3319 h−1, which was 1.8 times higher than that of the Ag/CDots/BiOBr with 0.3 wt% Ag (0.1812 h−1) (Fig. 6d). Besides, the degradation efficiency and the reaction rate constants of Ag/CDots/BiOBr (0.9 wt% Ag) were 1.3 and 1.9 times than those of Ag/BiOBr (0.9 wt% Ag). Therefore, the Ag/CDots/BiOBr with Ag contents at 0.9 wt%, possessed the highest photocatalytic performance in visible light irradiation. These results clearly indicated that the deposition of the Ag and CDots was beneficial for the enhancement of the photocatalytic performance of BiOBr.

Fig. 6e presented that DOC removal of the Ag/CDots/BiOBr increased 34.7% than that of BiOBr (27.6%) and the dechlorination efficiency of Ag/CDots/BiOBr was 80.2%, higher than that of BiOBr (38.8%). These results illustrated that the Ag/CDots/BiOBr photocatalytic powders exhibited a desirable mineralization and dechlorination capacity to 4-CP under visible-light irradiation in 6 h. Obviously, all the above experimental results further confirmed that the introduction of Ag and CDots into Ag/CDots/BiOBr system with suitable contents promoted the separation efficiency of electron/hole pairs and boosted the photocatalytic performances.

In addition to the photocatalytic activity, the reusability and stability are a general requirement for the practical applications. Following a simple washing and drying, the recycling reactions were examined by repeated photocatalytic experiments for the Ag/CDots/BiOBr composite. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 4-CP decreased slightly after five cycles (at 6.23%) (Fig. 6f), revealing that the composite possessed an excellent photostability.

Electrochemistry analysis and PL spectra were evaluated to explore the separation, migration, and trapping of photo-generated charge carriers. The photocurrent response curves of as-prepared samples were steady and reproducible during several on-off intermittent irradiation cycles (Fig. 7a). The photocurrent density of Ag/CDots/BiOBr possessed the highest among all the samples, which indicated that the Ag/CDots/BiOBr composite present the excellent ability of photo-generated charge carrier separation and superior electronic conductivity [46,47]. This might be attributed to the fact that the conjugated π structure of CDots, as the photo-generated electrons acceptors and separation centers, enhanced the separation efficiency of the photo-excited electron-hole pairs under the light illumination [5]. Meanwhile, it was noteworthy that the Ag nanoparticles could generate the electron-hole pairs under visible-light irradiation owing to the SPR effect and the accumulated electrons would transfer into the CB of BiOBr [17]. Thus, the co-modification by CDots and Ag could effectively improve separation of photo-generated charge carriers and boost the photocatalytic performance.

Further insight into the transfer properties of the photo-generated charge carriers can be further proved by EIS (Fig. 7b). As is known, the smaller impedance arc radius of the EIS Nyquist plot, the higher rate of interfacial charge carriers transfer and hence the higher photocatalytic activity [40]. The semicircle radius for the prepared materials increased in the order Ag/CDots/BiOBr < Ag/BiOBr < CDots/BiOBr < BiOBr, suggesting that fastest separation efficiency of photo-induced electrons and holes could be achieved over the Ag/CDots/BiOBr electrode [22].

The steady-state PL spectra in Fig. 7c presents that composites displayed three obvious emission peaks centered at around 362, 384 and 406 nm under the excitation wavelength at 300 nm. Apparently, the PL intensity of the Ag/CDots/BiOBr composite degreased significantly compared to the CDots/BiOBr, Ag/BiOBr and pristine BiOBr, revealing that CDots and Ag could effectively prevent the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes in Ag/CDots/BiOBr system [2,8,48]. The result of PL analysis was in accordance with the photocurrent and EIS analysis.

The identification of active species in the Ag/CDots/BiOBr system during the 4-CP degradation was performed by free radical trapping experiments, as showed in Fig. 8a. The obvious effect of the quenchers on removal efficiency of 4-CP decreased in the order of sodium oxalate (for h+) > TEMPOL (for O2 −) > Cr (VI) (for e−) > isopropanol (for OH) > Fe(II)-EDTA (for H2O2). Similarly, the result indicated that the active species of holes, O2 −, electrons, OH and H2O2 played the critical role in the photo-degradation of 4-CP.

In the basis of the above-mentioned results, the photocatalysis mechanisms of Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary catalysts are proposed in Fig. 8b. The electrons can be excited from the VB to the CB of BiOBr with the visible light irradiation, leaving the holes in the VB. Meanwhile, the metallic Ag can engender photo-generated electron/hole pairs owing to the SPR effect under visible light excitation [8,17,19]. The hot electrons in the metallic Ag can migrate to the CB of BiOBr. Besides, the delocalized conjugated structure of CDots acted as electron reservoir and transporter and the accumulated electrons can transfer from the VB of BiOBr to the CDots for enhancing the charge separation [5,8,24]. Simultaneously, the CDots enabled the upconvertion of the light from the longer wavelength to shorter wavelength, which in turn can be absorbed by the BiOBr for exciting additional electron/hole pairs [5,12,29]. Above all, the efficient separation of the photogenerated electron/hole pairs was improved at the interface of the Ag/CDots/BiOBr photocatalysts.

According to the DRS analysis (Fig. 5d), the VB and CB position of the Ag/CDots/BiOBr composite were determined to be 3.06 eV and 0.29 eV, respectively. The standard redox potentials of O2/H2O2 and OH/OH– were 0.682 and 2.38 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Thus, the photogenerated hot electrons could react with dissolved molecular O2 in water to generate H2O2 via the two-electron transfer (O2 + 2H+ + 2e− = H2O2), while the photogenerated holes could react with OH– to give rise to OH radical (h+ + OH– = OH) [5,24,49]. Theoretically, the CB of the prepared Ag/CDots/BiOBr composite was less positive than the standard reduction potential of O2/O2 – (−0.046 eV vs. NHE) and O2 – radical could not be produced [24,50]. Indeed, the CB position of the CDots occurred at about −1.16 eV according to previous studies [51], implying that the energetic electrons accumulated at the CDots could be captured by O2 to generate O2 – radical (O2 + e− = O2 −). The generation of H2O2 and activated O2 – radical could be further converted to generate OH radical [2,18,24]. The generation of the active species could effectively degrade the organic pollutants by the oxidation and reduction, which was consistent with the results of the free radical trapping experiments (Fig. 8a). As a result, the introduction of Ag and CDots for BiOBr made a great contribution to the enhancement in the photocatalytic activity.

A novel Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary photocatalyst has been successfully fabricated via IL [C16mim]Br assisted the solvothermal-reduction method. The Ag/CDots/BiOBr system (0.9 wt% Ag and 2 wt% CDots) exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity with 62.3% in mineralization and 81.5% in dechlorination capacity for 4-CP decomposition under visible light irradiation. The ternary photocatalyst had the reusability and stability for practical applications. The CDots as electron reservoir and transporter, prevent the recombination of photo-generated electron/hole pairs. Meanwhile, the up conversion properties of CDots improved the utilization of visible light. In addition, the SPR of the Ag nanoparticles could improve visible light absorbance and narrow band gap, then accelerated the efficient migration of photo-induced charges. The Ag nanoparticles and CDots co-doped on the BiOBr did not change the location CB of BiOBr while reduced the level of VB in the ternary system. The mechanism for the Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic performance was proposed and elucidated. These results suggest that Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary system is a promising photocatalyst for removing various contaminants from water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678270) for their financial support.
